title: 10 1 Harder Fourier Series
body:
TOPIC 10 : FOURIER SERIES II 10.1 SOME HARDER PROBLEMS

Suppose that a function f has period T = 2L. Then f may be approximated by the Fourier series ∞ X nπx nπx f (x) = a0 + (an cos + bn sin ) n=1 L L where the Fourier coefficients a0 , an , and bn are given by

RL a0 = 2L f (x) dx −L

RL nπx an = L1 f (x) cos dx (n = 1, 2, ) −L L

RL nπx bn = L1 f (x) sin dx (n = 1, 2, ) −L L

  x + 4, −1 ≤ x < 1; Example 1 Suppose that f (x) = f (x + 2), otherwise. 

Without integration show that an =0 and hence the Fourier series of f takes the form ∞ X nπx f (x) = a0 + bn sin n=1 L Find a0 by inspection and bn using Example 2 from the last lecture. To what value does the Fourier series converge at x = 1?

∞ 2 (−1)n+1 sin(nπx) P F f (x) = 4 + Converges to 4 at x = 1 F n=1 nπ

Example 2 A periodic voltage cos(t) is passed through a half-wave rectifier which clips the negative portion of the wave. Find the Fourier series of the resulting periodic function.

0.8 y 0.6 0.4

0.2

-15 -10 -5 0 5 10 15 -0.2 x

The integrals here will cause us all sorts of problems. Let us first prove two results which will be of use: π a) sin(θ + ) = cos(θ) b) cos(A) cos(B) = (cos(A + B) + cos(A − B)) Proof:

nπ 1 1 ∞ 2 cos( ) F f (x) = + cos(x) + P 2 cos(nx) F π 2 n=2 π(1 − n )

The first few terms of the series produce a function of this form:

1.5 y

0.5

-10 -5 0 5 10 -0.5 x

-1

We close with the note that when Fourier series are developed from a complex viewpoint we have ∞ X f (x) = cn einx n=−∞

with Zπ cn = f (x)e−inx dx. 2π −π

This is then the complex form of the Fourier series for f (x). The cn are the complex Fourier coefficients. Students are referred to the printed notes for a proof of the above results. We tend to focus on the real case in Math2019.
